examples.excerpt: After the introduction of transformer models in 2017, both patent and scientific publications increased greatly, with scientific publications exploding after the 2022 release of ChatGPT.
The share of GenAI patent publications among all AI patents has shown a slight uptick since 2017, from 4.2% to 6.1% in 2023, and will likely further increase in future.

The number of scientific publications has risen even more over the same period, from only around 100 in 2014 to more than 34,000 in 2023. In 2023 particularly, there has been a strong increase in scientific publications. It is likely that the release of very successful and popular GenAI models and tools in 2022 (ChatGPT, Stable Diffusion, LlaMA, etc.) has initiated a new wave of GenAI research.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Titanium is a metal with high industrial and commercial interest."},{"excerpt":"China dominates the patent filings in processes for the production of titanium dioxide from titanium, with 73 percent of all patents in our dataset being filed with China as the priority country – that is, the country where the first patent of each patent family is filed (Figure 10)."},{"excerpt":"The chloride process is only mentioned in 8 percent of patent families, although it provides 60 percent of the worldwide industrial production of titanium dioxide."}]}

examples.excerpt: Titanium is a metal with high industrial and commercial interest.

China dominates the patent filings in processes for the production of titanium dioxide from titanium, with 73 percent of all patents in our dataset being filed with China as the priority country – that is, the country where the first patent of each patent family is filed (Figure 10).
The chloride process is only mentioned in 8 percent of patent families, although it provides 60 percent of the worldwide industrial production of titanium dioxide.

raw_output: {"M":"CAGR = (C_{end} / C_{start})^{1/(Y_{end} - Y_{start})} - 1, where C_{start} and C_{end} are the distinct patent family counts in the start and end years Y_{start} and Y_{end} of the interval of interest.","T":"The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) quantifies the average yearly percentage change in the number of patent families over a specified period, assuming the growth compounds each year between the start and end years."}

parsed_output.M: CAGR = (C_{end} / C_{start})^{1/(Y_{end} - Y_{start})} - 1, where C_{start} and C_{end} are the distinct patent family counts in the start and end years Y_{start} and Y_{end} of the interval of interest.
parsed_output.T: The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) quantifies the average yearly percentage change in the number of patent families over a specified period, assuming the growth compounds each year between the start and end years.

examples.excerpt: The United States and China initially exhibit strong specialization (RSI = 1) but each has experienced a steady decline over time, with an RSI values falling to 0.6 and 0.3, respectively, by 2020–2024.

Patent growth in Prediction technologies by priority country shows that China experienced strong growth initially but slowed down in later years. The United States grew slowly at first and then underwent a significant decline. The Republic of Korea has shown steady growth, while India has seen a sharp rise over recent years.
Gree has consistently filed the most patents by far over the last 10 years whereas Haier is the leader of last 5 years.

Patent growth in Prediction technologies by priority country shows that China experienced strong growth initially but slowed down in later years (Figure 4.6). The United States grew slowly at first and then underwent a significant decline.

Periodic patent growth in Protection technologies reveals varied trends across countries. China and India lead with strong growth, particularly during the mid-period, but whereas India has sustained a high momentum, China experienced a decline after 2020.

Figure 5.5 shows that India and Canada show the strongest and most sustained specialization in Detection technologies, with consistently high focus. China and the United States demonstrate an initially strong engagement, but China's specialization has declined significantly over time, whereas that of the United States has stabilized after some fluctuation.

parsed_output.excerpt:
Key locations of inventors
China is at the forefront of global patenting activity in GenAI. China was responsible for more than 38,000 patent family publications between 2014 and 2023, based on the inventor addresses published on patents. Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field every year than all other countries combined.
With a total of around 6,300 patent families between 2014 and 2023, the US is the second most important research location for GenAI. The Asian countries of the Republic of Korea, Japan and India are also important research locations in GenAI, all ranking in the top 5 countries worldwide (third, fourth and fifth respectively) (Figure 16).

China was responsible for more than 38,000 patent family publications between 2014 and 2023, based on the inventor addresses published on patents. Key locations of inventors
China is at the forefront of global patenting activity in GenAI. China was responsible for more than 38,000 patent family publications between 2014 and 2023, based on the inventor addresses published on patents. Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field every year than all other countries combined.
With a total of around 6,300 patent families between 2014 and 2023, the US is the second most important research location for GenAI. These seven inventor locations account for the majority of patenting activity related to GenAI, contributing around 98 percent of the dataset, with a few contributions from other countries such as Canada, Israel and France.

than 38,000 patent family publications between 2014 and 2023, based on the inventor addresses

parsed_output.M: For any ordered pair of entities (entity, pair_entity) with start year y_start and end year y_end, let s_entity and s_pair be the start counts for entity and pair_entity, and e_entity and e_pair be the end counts. Then start_gap = |s_entity - s_pair|, end_gap = |e_entity - e_pair|, and gap_delta = end_gap - start_gap. The table records: entity, pair_entity, start_year, start_count (s_entity), end_year, end_count (e_entity), start_gap, end_gap, gap_delta.
parsed_output.T: The table lists each ordered pair of entities and shows how many items (e.g., patent families) each had at a start year and an end year. It then computes the absolute difference between the two entities at the start (start_gap) and at the end (end_gap), and finally the change in that difference (gap_delta). Rows are provided for both orderings of each pair, but the gap values are symmetric.

parsed_output.M:
For each row describing a pair of entities (e, p) with start year y_s, end year y_e, start counts c_s(e), c_s(p) and end counts c_e(e), c_e(p):
- start_gap = c_s(p) - c_s(e)

- gap_delta = end_gap - start_gap
- growth_rate(e) = (c_e(e) / c_s(e))^(1/(y_e - y_s)) - 1   (annualized growth)
- growth_rate(p) = (c_e(p) / c_s(p))^(1/(y_e - y_s)) - 1
- comparative_growth = growth_rate(p) - growth_rate(e)
parsed_output.T: The table quantifies, for each compared pair of entities, how many patent families each had at the start and end of the observation period, the difference between the two entities at those points (start_gap and end_gap), and how that difference changed over time (gap_delta). It also allows the computation of each entity’s annualized growth rate and the difference between the two growth rates, which corresponds to the comparative statements about faster or slower growth found in the accompanying text.

parsed_output.M: For each ordered pair (entity A, entity B): let start_year = sY, end_year = eY; let start_count_A = C_A_s, start_count_B = C_B_s, end_count_A = C_A_e, end_count_B = C_B_e. Compute start_gap = C_B_s − C_A_s, end_gap = C_B_e − C_A_e, and gap_delta = end_gap − start_gap.
parsed_output.T: The table presents a pairwise comparison of patent‑family activity between two entities over a defined period. For each direction (A→B and B→A) it lists the start and end years, the number of patent families each entity had at those years, the difference in counts (gap) between the counterpart and the focal entity at the start and at the end, and the change in that gap over time.

parsed_output.M: For each pair of entities (A,B) described in the text, identify the common start year Y_s and end year Y_e, and the counts of patent families for each entity at those years: N_A_s, N_A_e for A and N_B_s, N_B_e for B. Then generate two rows: one with entity=A, pair_entity=B and another with entity=B, pair_entity=A. For each row set start_year=Y_s, end_year=Y_e, start_count = N_entity_s, end_count = N_entity_e. Compute start_gap = |N_A_s - N_B_s|, end_gap = |N_A_e - N_B_e|, and gap_delta = end_gap - start_gap. The gap values are identical in both rows.
parsed_output.T: From the paragraph, extract the two compared countries (or entities) and the years at the beginning and end of the observation period. Record how many patent families each country had in the start year and in the end year. Then calculate the absolute difference between the two countries' start‑year counts (start_gap) and the absolute difference between their end‑year counts (end_gap). Finally, compute how much the gap changed (gap_delta = end_gap minus start_gap). Produce a table row for each direction of the pair, repeating the gap values for both rows.

parsed_output.M: For each ordered pair (A,B): start_year = Y_s, end_year = Y_e; start_count = c_A(Y_s), end_count = c_A(Y_e); start_gap = |c_A(Y_s) - c_B(Y_s)|; end_gap = |c_A(Y_e) - c_B(Y_e)|; gap_delta = end_gap - start_gap.
parsed_output.T: The table lists, for each pair of entities, the count for the first entity at the start and end years, the absolute difference (gap) between the two entities' counts at those years, and the change in that gap (gap_delta) over the period.

parsed_output.M: Let A and B be two entities. For a given start year Y_s and end year Y_e, let C_A_s and C_B_s be the start counts for A and B, and C_A_e and C_B_e be the end counts. Define start_gap = C_A_s - C_B_s, end_gap = C_A_e - C_B_e, and gap_delta = end_gap - start_gap. The table records (entity=A, pair_entity=B, start_year=Y_s, start_count=C_A_s, end_year=Y_e, end_count=C_A_e, start_gap, end_gap, gap_delta).
parsed_output.T: For every ordered pair of entities mentioned, the output table lists the start and end years, the patent‑family counts for the first entity, the difference between that entity’s count and the second entity’s count at the start (start_gap) and at the end (end_gap), and how that difference changed over the period (gap_delta). Both directions of each entity pair are included.

Patent activity
Between 2012 and 2022, there were 2,027 active patent families describing the use of titanium dioxide and titanium in ceramics (Figure 24). Patent activity shows a growing interest in ceramics as an industrial application of titanium since 2016, with a CAGR of +15 percent between 2014 and 2019.

raw_output: {"M":"Given a sequence of years y and the corresponding distinct patent family counts c(y), the average annual growth rate (AAGR) over the interval from year y0 to year yN is defined as AAGR = (c(yN)/c(y0))^{1/(yN - y0)} - 1. parsed_output.M: Given a sequence of years y and the corresponding distinct patent family counts c(y), the average annual growth rate (AAGR) over the interval from year y0 to year yN is defined as AAGR = (c(yN)/c(y0))^{1/(yN - y0)} - 1. The compound annual growth rate (CAGR) is expressed similarly. The data can be represented as a function f(y)=c(y) mapping each year to its patent family count.

Across the examples, these counts generally increase over time, often at high rates (e.g., around 45% per year), reflecting rapid growth in patent activity within those fields.

raw_output: {"M":"For a given domain, let P_y denote the distinct patent family count in year y. The average annual growth rate (AAGR) between years y1 and y2 is defined as AAGR = ((P_{y2} / P_{y1})^{1/(y2 - y1)}) - 1. The expected count in any year y can be modeled as P_y = P_{y0} * (1 + AAGR)^{(y - y0)} where y0 is a reference year.", "T":"Across the provided examples, the number of distinct patent families generally rises over time, often following an exponential‑like pattern with an average yearly increase of about 45% in many cases. This growth can be captured by the exponential model defined above, while noting that some domains may experience slower growth or a decline after reaching a peak."}

parsed_output.M: For a given domain, let P_y denote the distinct patent family count in year y. The average annual growth rate (AAGR) between years y1 and y2 is defined as AAGR = ((P_{y2} / P_{y1})^{1/(y2 - y1)}) - 1. The expected count in any year y can be modeled as P_y = P_{y0} * (1 + AAGR)^{(y - y0)} where y0 is a reference year.
parsed_output.T: Across the provided examples, the number of distinct patent families generally rises over time, often following an exponential‑like pattern with an average yearly increase of about 45% in many cases. This growth can be captured by the exponential model defined above, while noting that some domains may experience slower growth or a decline after reaching a peak.

parsed_output.M: For values V_0 at start year and V_N at end year over N years, the average annual growth rate (AAGR) = (V_N / V_0)^(1/N) - 1 (expressed as a decimal or percentage).
parsed_output.T: The average annual growth rate measures the typical yearly percentage change in patent‑family counts over a period, calculated by dividing the final count by the initial count, taking the Nth root (where N is the number of years elapsed), and subtracting one to express the result as a yearly growth percentage.

raw_output: {"M":"Let C_y denote the count of distinct patent families in year y for a given technology or region. The annual growth rate for year y (y>y₀) is g_y = (C_y - C_{y-1}) / C_{y-1}. The average annual growth rate over a period Y = {y₁,…,y_n} is G = (1/(n-1)) * Σ_{i=2}^{n} g_{y_i}.","T":"The examples show yearly time‑series of distinct GenAI‑related patent family counts for various domains (e.g., GANs, speech/voice/music, global GenAI) and regions (Japan, Korea). Across these series the typical pattern is a rapid increase starting around 2017—often with an average yearly growth of about 45 %—followed by either continued growth, stabilization, or a decline in later years depending on the specific technology or region."}

parsed_output.M: Let C_y denote the count of distinct patent families in year y for a given technology or region. The annual growth rate for year y (y>y₀) is g_y = (C_y - C_{y-1}) / C_{y-1}. The average annual growth rate over a period Y = {y₁,…,y_n} is G = (1/(n-1)) * Σ_{i=2}^{n} g_{y_i}.
parsed_output.T: The examples show yearly time‑series of distinct GenAI‑related patent family counts for various domains (e.g., GANs, speech/voice/music, global GenAI) and regions (Japan, Korea). Across these series the typical pattern is a rapid increase starting around 2017—often with an average yearly growth of about 45 %—followed by either continued growth, stabilization, or a decline in later years depending on the specific technology or region.

India has recorded even higher recent growth rates, propelling it to sixth place in the ranking, with 3,113 published patent families since 2020."},{"excerpt":"Over the past five years, India has shown a remarkable surge in patenting activity related to heavy-duty energy infrastructure, rising from 39 publications in 2019 to 449 in 2024. examples.excerpt: India has emerged as the fastest-growing innovation location in vehicle efficiency, showing a surge in patent publications from 66 in 2019 to 300 in 2024. This corresponds to a robust 35% CAGR. Sweden was the only other major research location to exhibit strong momentum, recording an average annual increase in published patent families of 11%. In contrast, patenting activity has largely stagnated over the past five years in Canada, China and Germany.

China has become the top patenting location for decarbonization technologies in heavy-duty road transport. Between 2000 and 2024, Chinese patent families expanded from just 11 in 2000 to around 7,300 in 2024, though Chinese inventors focus primarily on domestic patent protection. India has recorded even higher recent growth rates, propelling it to sixth place in the ranking, with 3,113 published patent families since 2020.

Over the past five years, India has shown a remarkable surge in patenting activity related to heavy-duty energy infrastructure, rising from 39 publications in 2019 to 449 in 2024. This corresponds to a compound annual growth rate of more than 60%, making India by far the fastest-growing innovation location in this field. Sweden and Canada also have strong momentum, recording average annual increases in published patent families of 24% and 22%, respectively. China, which remains the largest patenting country in terms of absolute numbers, has posted an annual growth rate of around 12% since 2019.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Since 2000, China has experienced the highest patent growth rates of any of the top 15 inventor locations. Between 2000 and 2019, Chinese patent families expanded by compound rate of 38% per year. However, China’s patent growth has slowed in recent years, leading to an annual growth rate of 14% between 2019 and 2024 (Figure 3.6). As the absolute volume of patents has grown significantly, percentage‑based growth has naturally levelled off. Meanwhile, rising overcapacity in China’s battery production has probably further dampened growth recently, with companies prioritizing cost reduction and efficiency over research and development activities. Consequently, since 2019, India, Sweden, Canada and France have surpassed China’s patent growth rates, albeit from a much lower starting point."},{"excerpt":"Patent growth and specialization\nAnalysis of patenting activity over the past five years reveals significant geographical divergence (Figure 5.5). India has emerged as the fastest‑growing innovation location in vehicle efficiency, showing a surge in patent publications from 66 in 2019 to 300 in 2024. This corresponds to a robust 35% CAGR. Sweden was the only other major research location to exhibit strong momentum, recording an average annual increase in published patent families of 11%.\nIn contrast, patenting activity has largely stagnated over the past five years in Canada, China and Germany."},{"excerpt":"Sweden has achieved the highest patent growth rate in the area of green digitalization, at a compound annual growth of 27% per annum (Figure 6.4). India, Israel, the Netherlands and China have also demonstrated notable double‑digit growth. In contrast, patenting activity has stagnated in the United States since 2019 and declined in Germany, the United Kingdom, France and Austria."}]}

examples.excerpt: Since 2000, China has experienced the highest patent growth rates of any of the top 15 inventor locations. Between 2000 and 2019, Chinese patent families expanded by compound rate of 38% per year. However, China’s patent growth has slowed in recent years, leading to an annual growth rate of 14% between 2019 and 2024 (Figure 3.6). As the absolute volume of patents has grown significantly, percentage‑based growth has naturally levelled off. Meanwhile, rising overcapacity in China’s battery production has probably further dampened growth recently, with companies prioritizing cost reduction and efficiency over research and development activities. Consequently, since 2019, India, Sweden, Canada and France have surpassed China’s patent growth rates, albeit from a much lower starting point.

Patent growth and specialization
Analysis of patenting activity over the past five years reveals significant geographical divergence (Figure 5.5). India has emerged as the fastest‑growing innovation location in vehicle efficiency, showing a surge in patent publications from 66 in 2019 to 300 in 2024. This corresponds to a robust 35% CAGR. Sweden was the only other major research location to exhibit strong momentum, recording an average annual increase in published patent families of 11%.
In contrast, patenting activity has largely stagnated over the past five years in Canada, China and Germany.

Sweden has achieved the highest patent growth rate in the area of green digitalization, at a compound annual growth of 27% per annum (Figure 6.4). India, Israel, the Netherlands and China have also demonstrated notable double‑digit growth. In contrast, patenting activity has stagnated in the United States since 2019 and declined in Germany, the United Kingdom, France and Austria.

Sweden and Germany demonstrate exceptionally high relative specialization across all four low‑emission energy source types, whereas China’s RSI is below average."}]}

Between 2000 and 2019, Chinese patent families expanded by compound rate of 38% per year. However, China’s patent growth has slowed in recent years, leading to an annual growth rate of 14% between 2019 and 2024 (Figure 3.6). As the absolute volume of patents has grown significantly, percentage-based growth has naturally levelled off. Consequently, since 2019, India, Sweden, Canada and France have surpassed China’s patent growth rates, albeit from a much lower starting point.
Consequently, since 2019, India, Sweden, Canada and France have surpassed China’s patent growth rates, albeit from a much lower starting point.

China’s level of specialization is lower than that of other leading research locations. Sweden and Germany demonstrate exceptionally high relative specialization across all four low‑emission energy source types, whereas China’s RSI is below average.

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"the GenAI share of all AI patents has been increasing (from 4.2% in 2017 to 6.1% in 2023)"},{"excerpt":"India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small."},{"excerpt":"Patents in the use of GenAI in energy management have seen the largest growth, both since 2018 and also in the last three years, with applications in agriculture also growing more in the last three years. In contrast, the number of patent families has stagnated or even declined in certain application areas such as telecommunications, military, arts and humanities or industrial property/law/social and behavioral sciences (Figure 27)."}]}

examples.excerpt: the GenAI share of all AI patents has been increasing (from 4.2% in 2017 to 6.1% in 2023)
India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small.

Patents in the use of GenAI in energy management have seen the largest growth, both since 2018 and also in the last three years, with applications in agriculture also growing more in the last three years. In contrast, the number of patent families has stagnated or even declined in certain application areas such as telecommunications, military, arts and humanities or industrial property/law/social and behavioral sciences (Figure 27).

raw_output: {"examples":[{"excerpt":"Not only does China have the largest share of all patent families published globally but also is growing at the rate of 50% per year. However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small."},{"excerpt":"In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023."},{"excerpt":"The Republic of Korea has also achieved high growth rates in their GenAI patent families since 2014. By contrast, Japan's GenAI patent families have risen only a little more than 10% per year on average."}]}

examples.excerpt: Not only does China have the largest share of all patent families published globally but also is growing at the rate of 50% per year. However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small.

In contrast, diffusion models and LLMs show much higher growth rates over the last three years, with the number of patent families for diffusion models increasing from 18 in 2020 to 441 in 2023 and for LLMs increasing from 53 in 2020 to 881 in 2023.

The Republic of Korea has also achieved high growth rates in their GenAI patent families since 2014. By contrast, Japan's GenAI patent families have risen only a little more than 10% per year on average.

examples.excerpt: Since 2017, China has published more patent families in this field every year than all other countries combined.
However, India has an even higher growth rate of 56% per year even though its world share is still small.
Scientific publications with at least one affiliation in the US received significantly more citations globally than those with at least one Chinese affiliation.
